edges.description: Vasospastic or hypoxic injury is thought to trigger focal loss of medial smooth muscle integrity.
Arterial gaps and medial weakening produce dilatation, aneurysm, and dissection.
Arterial-medial gap junctions can evolve into arterial dissection.
Flow-limiting stenosis, occlusion, dissection, and rupture can cause ischemic organ injury or life-threatening bleeding.
Arterial rupture can cause life-threatening intra-abdominal bleeding.

nodes.description: SAM produces multifocal visceral arterial aneurysms, often involving renal, superior mesenteric, celiac, hepatic, and splenic arteries.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental arterial mediolysis (SAM): Systematic review and analysis of 143 cases.
evidence.snippet: The most commonly affected vessels were the superior mesenteric (53%), hepatic (45%), celiac (36%), renal (26%), and splenic (25%) arteries with aneurysm (76%), dissection (61%), and arterial rupture (46%).

nodes.description: SAM commonly produces visceral arterial dissections, a distinct vascular lesion from aneurysm or dilatation.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental Arterial Mediolysis: Abdominal Imaging of and Disease Course in 111 Patients.
evidence.snippet: The most common imaging findings were dissection (86%), aneurysm (57%), beading or webs (28%), occlusion (19%), and a rind or wall thickening (15%).

nodes.description: Dissection, stenosis, or occlusion in visceral or renal arteries can cause ischemic injury to supplied organs.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental arterial mediolysis and its mimickers: a case report and review of the literature
evidence.snippet: Abdominal computerized tomography showed arterial thickening, arterial dilatations, and dissections of the splenic and renal arteries, leading to organ ischemia.

nodes.description: Rupture of affected arteries can cause intra-abdominal bleeding or severe hemorrhage.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental arterial mediolysis (SAM): Systematic review and analysis of 143 cases.
evidence.snippet: Abdominal pain (80%) and intraabdominal bleeding (50%) were the most common presenting symptoms.

nodes.description: Lytic loss of arterial medial smooth muscle weakens the vessel wall and creates arterial gaps that evolve into dilatation, aneurysm, dissection, stenosis, or occlusion.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental arterial mediolysis: course, sequelae, prognosis, and pathologic-radiologic correlation.
evidence.snippet: Pathologic correlations reveal that lytic loss of medial muscle causes arterial dilatation, dilated arterial gaps form aneurysms, dissections develop at arterial-medial gap junctions or from reparative granulation tissue and reparative alterations, and thrombi cause stenosis and occlusions.

nodes.description: SAM is hypothesized to begin with a vasospastic arterial insult that injures the arterial media rather than a primary inflammatory arteritis or atherosclerotic process.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental arterial mediolysis: course, sequelae, prognosis, and pathologic-radiologic correlation.
evidence.snippet: Segmental arterial mediolysis is a vascular disease of putative vasospastic origin that causes massive hemorrhages.

nodes.description: After the injurious phase, reparative granulation tissue, fibrosis, and thrombus can remodel the arterial wall, sometimes stabilizing lesions and sometimes producing stenosis or occlusion.

evidence.reference_title: Segmental arterial mediolysis: course, sequelae, prognosis, and pathologic-radiologic correlation.
evidence.snippet: The most common radiologic findings at onset are aneurysms, arterial dilatation, and occlusions, while dissections and stenotic lesions often are delayed.
